title: Founders vs. Managers: The Transition That Kills Companies

source.original: David Senra's Conversation with Marc Andreessen (@pmarca) – Founders, Tech as Progress, and the Zero Introspection Mindset

body_html:
Marc Andreessen draws a sharp and recurring distinction between founders and managers, arguing that the transition from one to the other represents a critical—and often destructive—inflection point in a company's life. Founders create; managers optimize. Both are necessary, but the shift from founder energy to managerial control frequently erodes the very dynamism that made the company valuable in the first place.

This isn't just abstract theory. Andreessen points to HP and Intel as historical case studies in what happens when founder-led cultures give way to professional management. In both cases, the companies achieved enormous success under their founders, only to gradually lose their innovative edge as layers of management introduced process, risk aversion, and incrementalism in place of bold vision.

The founder-to-manager transition is often celebrated as a sign of maturity—a company "growing up." But Andreessen's view is more skeptical. He sees it as a moment where the organization's immune system starts attacking its own source of vitality. Managers are trained to reduce variance, but variance is precisely where breakthrough value comes from.

This tension is structural, not personal. Many managers are extraordinarily talented. The problem is that the managerial function is inherently conservative, designed to protect and extend what already exists rather than to disrupt it. When this function dominates, the company begins to coast on accumulated advantage rather than generating new advantage.

For anyone building or investing in companies, this framework is a useful lens. The question isn't just "who's running the company?" but "what kind of energy is running the company?" The answer to that question often predicts whether the next decade will be one of creation or slow decline.
